🗞️ Newsletter: 👉 Subscribe here

Eli Drinkwitz gets me. We're on the same wavelength. I enjoy pickleball, but, the first thing that went through my mind was the stress on my knees and how I'd be way better off playing doubles. Is it time for football coaches to ban their players from pickleball?

Are you a coach who has banned your athletes from the sport? Let me know: joe.kinsey@outkick.com

• John H. says: One can confidently predict increased vehicle prevalence in a given geography via identification of contributing factors. Let me explain. In the Midwest, there is a higher prevalence of large trucks due to the widespread farming industry that requires expanded space for transport. Conversely, in urban settings such as New York City, there is a higher prevalence of small cars due to the limited space for parking and travel. And then there is Miami, a space-limited urban environment which has a high prevalence of large trucks due to the high prevalence of small men.

– Chris B. keeps it simple: In Florida on I-75: "Speed Limit 70"

Kinsey: Well played.

– Kevin in Gibsonia has one: Interstate 90 in New York about 20 mile past the PA border..."Correctional facility ahead, Don't pick up hitchhikers."

– Drew in Katy, TX was all over this: My wife and I moved to South Louisiana in 1990 and thoroughly enjoyed living there for 9 years.  We loved the people.  The food is fabulous.  The fishing is fantastic, etc.  But the day that we drove from Houston to move to New Orleans on I-10, I saw this sign just as we crossed the Sabine River bridge into Louisiana.  The yellow caution sign read "SUBSTANDARD ROAD AHEAD".  I had no idea that it applied to every road and street in the state. 

– Brad S. gives us a report: We had a party for my son and 16 of his 14-15 year old friends yesterday. We did pizza and hot dogs when the bulk of the pool party folks were there. No issues, except for a few kids constantly f-bombing. We let them know that wasn't welcome. The other kids started 'policing' the foul-mouthed kids, so it stopped.

When the party started to wind down, the six kids sleeping over were hungry. It was 10:45 and choices were limited - and they had cleaned us out of our food. Taco Bell was their choice! I insisted NO LETTUCE!

Two kids grabbed everyone's screenshots and we headed over. We got there before they closed the lobby and the two went in and used the kiosk to order. I gave them my card to pay and an alert came across my phone minutes later for a $120 charge... at Taco Bell!

If I had sent $120 worth of Taco Bell to Ethiopia in the mid 80s, there wouldn't have been the need for a Live Aid concert! I started to wonder if I would have enough room in my SUV to fit all of it! 25 minutes later they came out with four huge bags. I'm grateful to my plumber that my plumbing is in great shape! I hope.

UPDATE:

At 2AM, I had to stop them from going outside to play hacky-sak (still a thing?) I eventually got them quieted down. Mrs. S and daughter are finally sleeping. But the teenage animals are still seeking food.

4AM. They've literally eaten everything: Pasta salad, fruit, frozen chicken nuggets from the freezer, lunch meat... it's all gone. I've long transitioned from finishing work stuff to watching Tom Snyder interviewing Glen Campbell on YouTube, etc. I'm an older dad, but I'm getting too old for this sh*t.

I never thought I'd say this, but I miss the Chuck E Cheeze days.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

SIGN UP TO GET THE LATEST SPORTS NEWS

Talk about an end of an era, Ravech has worked for ESPN since 1993. If there's a silver lining, it's that Ravech is nearing Social Security age. Wiki has him at 61 or 62. I know, it's wild, but we're all getting older by the day.

Time moves on.

And with that, let's get the day rolling. Get busy living.

item.title: Trump's backyard is becoming America's biggest magnet for taxpayer wealth
item.description: Palm Beach County attracted over $3 billion in taxable income from interstate migrants, while Los Angeles County lost nearly $1.9 billion in AGI.
item.content:encoded:
One red county is emerging as America's biggest winner in the race for people and prosperity, attracting more wealth from migrating taxpayers than any other county in the nation.

The latest IRS migration data show President Donald Trump's hometown of Palm Beach County, Florida, attracted more than $3 billion in taxable income from households moving in from other states, the largest gain of any county in the nation.

Already one of the nation's wealthiest areas, Palm Beach is also home to Trump's Mar-a-Lago estate, which has been dubbed the Southern White House. The sunny Florida enclave has become an increasingly high-profile destination for political donors, business leaders and affluent residents, alongside Florida's broader influx of high-income households.

THE RED STATES RACING AHEAD IN AMERICA’S POWERFUL WEALTH BOOM — AND THE STATES FALLING BEHIND

The latest IRS figures suggest that trend continues to favor lower-tax, fast-growing Sun Belt states over many of the nation's largest coastal counties.

The findings offer one of the clearest snapshots yet of where Americans, and their money, are moving. That's important because the migration of higher-income households can have an outsized economic impact. They contribute more than just new neighbors, bringing paychecks, investments, businesses and spending power that can strengthen local economies while leaving behind smaller tax bases in the places they leave.

The migration wasn't limited to Palm Beach. 

Neighboring Collier County recorded the nation's second-largest net gain in adjusted gross income at roughly $2.25 billion, with nearly all of that growth also driven by newcomers from outside Florida. Adjusted gross income (AGI) measures the taxable income reported on federal tax returns, making it one of the clearest indicators of how much earning power is moving from one place to another.

Because higher-income households pay a disproportionate share of federal income taxes, changes in adjusted gross income can have an outsized effect on local tax bases, consumer spending and economic growth.

BILLIONS IN TAXPAYER INCOME ARE LEAVING TWO ICONIC STATES — AS A NEW ECONOMIC MAP EMERGES

Meanwhile, some of the nation's largest metropolitan counties continued to lose both taxpayers and wealth through interstate migration.

Los Angeles County, home to Hollywood and one of the nation's largest economic engines, recorded the nation's largest net loss of tax filers, shedding 17,496 returns. Those departing taxpayers took nearly $1.9 billion in adjusted gross income with them as they relocated to other states.

Other major counties in California and New York also recorded significant taxpayer losses. Queens County, New York, lost 17,109 tax filers, followed by the Bronx (-16,319), Orange County, California (-11,618) and Suffolk County, New York (-10,434), highlighting the continued outflow from some of the nation's largest coastal population centers.

One county, however, stood out for a different reason.

Manhattan recorded the nation's largest net gain in interstate tax filers while simultaneously losing about $922 million in adjusted gross income, suggesting many of the households arriving earned less than the higher-income taxpayers who departed.

AMERICA'S TOP DESTINATION FOR NEW TAXPAYERS IS QUIETLY GETTING POORER, IRS DATA REVEALS

The contrast underscores that population growth and wealth growth are not always the same thing.

While Manhattan added more new tax filers than any other county, Palm Beach and Collier captured billions more in taxable income, illustrating how the migration of affluent households can reshape local economies even when fewer people move.

Trump officially moved his main residence from his longtime home of Manhattan to his estate in Palm Beach in 2019, while he was serving his first term in office. At the time of the move, he slammed New York City's liberal policies and claimed he was "treated very badly" by political leaders in the state despite paying millions in taxes.

In the years after his move, many of Trump's wealthy inner circle also relocated to Palm Beach and some surrounding Florida counties. The Sunshine State has no state income tax and levies neither estate nor inheritance taxes, while New York’s top state income tax rate exceeds 10%.

Taken together, the IRS figures suggest America's economic center of gravity continues to shift south.

As billions of dollars in taxable income flow toward Florida, Texas and other Sun Belt states, many of the nation's traditional coastal economic powerhouses are losing not just residents — but some of their highest-earning taxpayers.

item.title: Dem Senate hopeful ripped as 'absentee legislator' after missing nearly 60% of votes
item.description: Ashley Hinson blasted Josh Turek for missing 58% of Iowa House votes while collecting over $20,000 in per diem as the Iowa Senate race heats up.
item.content:encoded:
A Democrat Senate hopeful viewed as critical to Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer’s bid to retake Senate control is drawing criticism over his attendance record.

Rep. Ashley Hinson, R-Iowa, the Republican Senate nominee, blasted her Democratic opponent, state Rep. Josh Turek, D-Iowa, for missing 58% of House votes during the 2026 session while receiving tens of thousands of dollars in taxpayer benefits given to state legislators.

The absences, Hinson argues, are a black mark on Turek, a two-time Paralympic gold medalist, who is seeking to flip the Republican-held seat in November’s midterm elections.

"The truth is out. He's an absentee legislator," Hinson told Fox News Digital in a recent interview. "He accepted more than $20,000 in per diem benefits, but hardly showed up."

TRUMP-ENDORSED HINSON SECURES IOWA SENATE NOMINATION AS DEMOCRATS EYE GOP SEAT

Hinson’s comments came after Fox News Digital previously reported that Turek missed 261 of the chamber’s votes between Jan. 12 and May 3, amounting to a nearly 60% absence rate.

Turek, who has served in the Iowa legislature since 2023, spent much of that period locked in a contentious Democratic primary against a progressive backed by Sen. Elizabeth Warren, D-Mass.

Under Iowa state law, legislators receive a tax-free per diem of $201 for each day the legislature is in session. The per diem was paid for 100 legislative days this year, meaning Turek collected $20,100.

"He was skipping votes to attend ritzy fundraisers," Hinson said, referring to a report in The Washington Free Beacon that found Turek missed a vote in May on an appropriations bill providing funding for veterans’ assistance while attending a fundraiser hosted by the nonprofit side of the Schumer-aligned group, VoteVets.

"He was skipping votes to support farmers through the Iowa Farm Bill," she continued.

Hinson, who has cast the hotly contested race to succeed Sen. Joni Ernst, R-Iowa, as a contrast between her record and Turek's, touted her own attendance numbers.

"I have a 98.8% voting record in Congress," Hinson told Fox News Digital. "Iowans need a senator who's going to show up and be their advocate."

Asked for comment by Fox News Digital, the Turek campaign sharply criticized Hinson's own record.

"Ashley Hinson is a multi-millionaire who skipped votes on the annual defense funding bill to fundraise and wasted $50,000 in taxpayer funds to pay for her luxury D.C. apartment," a spokesperson for the Turek campaign said.

Hinson, who serves on the House Appropriations Committee, was absent during a portion of the panel's markup for this year's defense appropriations bill. That measure has yet to hit the House floor.

Under House rules, lawmakers are eligible for taxpayer-funded reimbursements for certain housing expenses while in Washington under a program adopted in 2023.

FOX NEWS POLL: A CLOSE SENATE CONTEST IS BREWING IN IOWA

The spat comes as Hinson and Turek appear locked in a dead heat, according to recent polling.

Though the Republican-leaning state has not elected a Democratic senator in nearly two decades, some Democrats argue that a poor political environment for Republicans, primarily driven by voter discontent over the economy and the cost of living, has put the open seat in play.

A Fox News poll released earlier in July found that Hinson trailed Turek 46% to 50% within the survey’s margin of error. The survey also showed that President Donald Trump has a net favorability rating of minus 13 points in Iowa, with just 42% of Iowans viewing him favorably.

Asked about the survey showing a statistical tie, Hinson largely brushed aside the numbers, saying she "always run[s] like [she's] 10 points behind" and vowed to "earn every single vote."

"I understand we're in a tough time for our country," Hinson told Fox News Digital. "I'm a minivan-driving mom, and I understand it's tough to keep those groceries stocked. It's tough to fill up the minivan with gas, and times are tough."

"My commitment is to continue to get out there and hear directly from Iowans and tell their stories in Washington so we can make sure we're actually getting the government out of the way and lowering costs for Iowa working families," she continued.

"Iowans just want someone who's common sense, who can relate to what they're going through," Hinson said. "And I think I will bring that to this election and win in November."

Hinson is endorsed by Trump and has represented a northeastern Iowa seat that includes Cedar Rapids since 2021. Before serving in Congress, she served in the state legislature and was a broadcast journalist.

The nonpartisan Cook Political Report rates the contest as "Lean Republican" after shifting the race toward Democrats following Turek's primary win.

Leo Briceno contributed to this report.

item.title: Fox News Power Rankings: Voters say they’re in economic pain, but will Democrats gain?
item.description: A majority now disapprove of Trump's handling of the economy, with healthcare and housing topping voter cost concerns ahead of the 2026 midterms.
item.content:encoded:
About a year and a half ago, President Trump ended his comeback campaign by telling a Michigan audience he would make America affordable again.

Voters listened. Trump swept the swing states, made inroads into Democratic strongholds, and by the time the counting was done, had posted a stronger performance in every state than he had four years prior.

The economy defined that election, as it does every election. Now, voters are saying something different: a majority disapprove of Trump’s handling of the economy, and their discontent goes deeper than tariffs and gas prices. Most Americans feel like the system is stacked against them.

FOX NEWS POLL: MOST RATE THE ECONOMY NEGATIVELY, INCLUDING HALF OF REPUBLICANS

Support for Trump on the economy has declined. His net approval has dropped 13 points to -37 between March last year and June, according to Fox News surveys; it’s down 24 points to -32 over a similar period in Washington Post/Ipsos polls, with the latest released just last week.

The president’s signature policies play a role in voter frustration, though to a lesser extent than headlines suggest. The cost of living is the most important factor in 46% of voters’ minds, and goods impacted by tariffs and the Iran war, including food (18%) and gas (15%), are in their top five cost concerns. But it’s healthcare (28%) and housing (19%) that top the list, according to a Reuters/Ipsos survey conducted in June.

That may explain why so many Americans are unhappy not just with the state of the economy, but the foundations underpinning it. Large majorities of Americans say the country’s political and economic systems are stacked against them, including 72% of middle-class and 86% of working-class adults. Those figures are each up by more than 30 points from a decade ago.

FOX NEWS POLL: ECONOMIC GLOOM, TRUMP RATINGS SIGNAL TOUGH GOP MIDTERM PATH

As the party out of office, Democrats are well-positioned to take advantage of voters’ economic pain. The question is whether voters think the party is offering a remedy.

The party has an edge by some measures:

TRUMP VS HISTORY: HOW PRESIDENT'S POLL NUMBERS COMPARE TO BIDEN, OBAMA, BUSH AHEAD OF MIDTERMS

These are not large gaps, suggesting that while voters don’t like the status quo, they’re not convinced that Democrats will solve their problems. It’s also too early to tell which version of the party’s message will resonate with voters. Add the GOP’s redistricting wins and favorable Senate map, and you get a midterm electorate that feels blue, but won’t necessarily vote blue.

The Senate is a coin flip. 

Assuming Democrats and Republicans win every seat leaning their way, they will each take home 48 seats, with four toss-up seats to determine the majority. Those are Alaska, Iowa, Maine and Ohio.

("Lean D" and "Lean R" seats are still highly competitive, as revealed in this Fox News Power Rankings explainer.)

The Democrats have a mountain to climb. Not only do they need to win three of the four toss-up races to reach 51 seats, they also need to hold on in Georgia, Michigan and New Hampshire, and flip North Carolina, which lean Democratic but are highly competitive.

Six of those eight states voted for Trump in 2024, and half of them by double digits, for an overall average of 7.8 points.

Recent history shows us that even when most states must go in one direction for a challenger to win, it doesn’t take a wave election for that to happen. Trump won the national vote by 1.5 points in his comeback victory, but he swept all seven of the swing states.

The same could happen in November: lean Democratic victories in key races, but a sweep in the states needed to flip the Senate majority. On a good night for Republicans, the same in reverse: many close wins and, based on this forecast, only a loss of one seat in the Senate.

Michigan

Michigan is the most compelling race on the map. If progressive candidate Abdul El-Sayed wins the upcoming Democratic primary, the question will be whether a left-wing economic populist message, led by Medicare for All, appeals to swing state voters. If moderate Rep. Haley Stevens gets the primary win, then her challenge will be to unite her party and prove that a center-left, anti-Trump message is what voters want.

In this political environment, Michigan is a Lean D state. It hasn’t voted for a Republican for Senate in 30 years. The margin of victory for Democrats has narrowed, but they held this seat just two years ago with an electorate that leaned Republican.

Presumptive GOP nominee and former Rep. Mike Rogers is about as ideal a candidate as his party could hope for, but his narrow loss in 2024 is a reminder that even with favorable conditions, the party still fell short.

There isn’t yet any polling evidence that El-Sayed would be a weak candidate in a general election, nor that Stevens would jeopardize what would normally be a Democratic pickup. This race starts at Lean D.

Iowa

Iowa has seemed firmly Republican since 2016, when white working-class voters turned out in droves for Trump.

They have remained loyal to the president, but overall, Iowa’s voters don’t look that different than Alaska or Ohio, two other states that both parties believe are ultra-competitive this year.

In the latest Fox News Poll, Democratic Senate candidate and State Rep. Josh Turek had a four-point edge at 50% to Republican Rep. Ashley Hinson at 46%. Turek’s counterpart for governor opened up a nine-point lead in the same poll. Other surveys show a tighter race, but all of these numbers spell out a toss-up election, which is where the state sits in this forecast.

Texas

The Lone Star State is Likely R on the fundamentals: the GOP has won every Senate race here since 1990, and though former Democratic Rep. Beto O’Rourke came close in 2018 (he lost by 2.6 points), the state clearly favors the Republican brand.

Democrats like their challenger, James Talarico, believe that his opponent, Attorney General Ken Paxton, is weak because of several scandals, and hope that Talarico’s self-described "cringy" comments won’t land as badly as O’Rourke’s did eight years prior.

The Democratic state representative has also enjoyed some tight polling in recent weeks, though he and Paxton are both several points short of a majority. This race starts at Lean R.

Democrats lead in the House, but victory is not guaranteed

The president’s party has lost an average of 25 U.S. House seats in Trump-era midterm elections. Given that Democrats only have an edge on the generic ballot and in party affiliation, so far, they look like they’re on track for an "average" night.

Republican redistricting wins change that calculation. The GOP is likely to bank about a dozen seats from new district boundaries drawn by Republican-controlled state legislatures, up to nine of those in Florida and Texas alone.

Democratic Gov. Gavin Newsom scored his party an extra three to five seats after voters approved his gerrymandering referendum, but overall, the GOP won this battle in what now looks like a redistricting "forever war."

Together, this adds up to a small Democratic lead in the House. They start with 211 races in their categories, or seven short of the 218 they need for a majority. If they win more than a handful of toss-up races, they will have enough to flip the chamber.

There is enough room for the Republicans to maintain control on a good night for the party. Since toss-up races tend to be won and lost together, they could even improve on the slim majority they currently enjoy.

More on that tomorrow, plus a seat-by-seat breakdown of the House forecast, and a close look at some of the districts that will define the race to 218.

item.title: Mullin reveals DHS brought down nearly 2,000 drones during World Cup security efforts
item.description: Markwayne Mullin says Homeland Security intercepted nearly 2,000 drones during the FIFA World Cup, some before operators could even leave their cars.
item.content:encoded:
Federal authorities became so adept at hunting down drones during the FIFA World Cup that some drones were intercepted before operators could remove them from cars, Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin revealed this week. He said security teams brought down nearly 2,000 drones over the course of the tournament.

"We got so good at [intercepting them], we started being able to bring them down before they even got out of the trunk of a car," Mullin said Monday on "The Will Cain Show."

"We started being able to recognize where they're at."

FEDS CHARGE FOUR AS WORLD CUP DRONE CRACKDOWN TOPS 400 SEIZURES ACROSS US HOST CITIES NATIONWIDE

Many of the drones belonged to spectators hoping for a bird's-eye view of the matches. But Mullin said others raised more serious concerns, with authorities believing some operators were intentionally probing security perimeters around stadiums and fan festivals.

"Some of them we felt like was actual a threat, that they were testing the perimeter. Those are some of the drones that we confiscated," he said.

"Some were licensed individuals that knew better, but they were trying to push it. We also confiscated those, but I think people were trying to see where our perimeters was or what our ability was."

PRESIDENT TRUMP STEALS PART OF SPOTLIGHT AS SPAIN CELEBRATES WORLD CUP TITLE OVER ARGENTINA

Mullin said authorities had "technology dialed in" to the point that security teams were able to locate operators before many drones ever took flight, even as operators launched drones from outside the established perimeter.

According to Mullin, the established no-fly zones extended three miles and 3,000 feet around stadiums, along with one-mile and 1,000-foot restrictions surrounding FIFA Fan Festivals. Security teams averaged more than 30 intercepted drones per match, he said, not including additional incidents around fan festivals and after games concluded.

Per Mullin's estimates, authorities confiscated roughly 700 drones and brought down nearly 2,000.

But rogue drones weren't the only threat authorities faced during the tournament. Mullin also disclosed that federal, state and local officials thwarted what he described as one imminent threat targeting a World Cup Fan Fest.

"I can't give you the location of it, but the threat was about using vehicles," he told Cain.

"It was specifically towards a fan fest and, fortunately enough, we were able to have coordination with federal partnerships."

item.title: Congress to put American Bar Association in hot seat over DEI rules tied to law school accreditation
item.description: Rep. Brandon Gill, R-Texas, called a hearing on Wednesday to investigate DEI's influence on law schools and the profession. The top witness is Michelle A. Behnke, the president of the American Bar Association.
item.content:encoded:
Congress is calling a hearing to examine whether the American Bar Association (ABA) has used its dominant role in law school accreditation to push diversity, equity and inclusion policies throughout the legal profession.

Rep. Brandon Gill, R-Texas, chairman of the House Oversight Committee’s Task Force on Defending Constitutional Rights and Exposing Constitutional Abuses, scheduled Wednesday’s hearing to question ABA President Michelle A. Behnke about the organization’s influence over law school accreditation.

"DEI is a cancerous ideology that promotes unlawful discrimination and steals opportunities from Americans who would otherwise earn jobs and law school admissions spots on merit," Gill said in a statement. "The ABA has abused its accreditation power to force harmfully racist and often illegal DEI policies and coursework in law schools across the nation."

Gill added that the ABA's "promotion of DEI has created poorly trained lawyers who prioritize political activism over following the law."

CALIFORNIA COUNTY APPROVES REPARATIONS PLAN AS NEIGHBORING SCHOOL DISTRICT ‘BLACK THRIVING’ EFFORT STALLS

The task force has laid out the goal of exploring "how Congress can help permanently eliminate DEI policies within American institutions, including the legal profession and academy."

The ABA’s accreditation system gives it considerable influence over legal education. Its Council of the Section of Legal Education and Admissions to the Bar is the only accreditor of Juris Doctor programs recognized by the U.S. Department of Education, and graduation from an ABA-approved law school is the standard path to taking the bar exam in most states.

In the hearing, Behnke is expected to argue that the council operates independently of the broader ABA, a distinction she made clear in a June 1 statement.

Despite the council's decision in May to repeal certain diversity and inclusion standards for law schools, Behnke said the ABA still has an "unwavering" commitment to DEI, comments that Gill and other committee members are likely to scrutinize.

RED-STATE SENATOR DROPS HAMMER ON DEM MAYOR OVER NEW ‘WOKE’ DEI ORDINANCE WHILE VIOLENT CRIME SURGES

"The legal profession is strongest when it draws on the experiences, talents and perspectives of people from every background and every community," Behnke said last month. "Diversity does not weaken our profession. It deepens it. It broadens understanding, strengthens public trust and helps ensure the law serves all people fairly and fully."

President Donald Trump has put pressure on law firms and other American companies to end DEI practices.

However, even before Trump returned to the White House last January, the ABA accreditation council was already considering revising Standard 206 of the ABA Standards and Rules of Procedure for Approval of Law Schools. A November 2024 revision called for removing sections including a requirement for law schools to "demonstrate by concrete action a commitment to diversity and inclusion by having a faculty and staff that are diverse with respect to gender, race, and ethnicity."

In August 2024, the council began revising Standard 206 to remove language expressly permitting the consideration of race and ethnicity in admissions. 

The revisions also clarified that law schools were not required to consider race or other identity characteristics in individual admissions decisions.

In May 2026, the ABA’s accreditation council voted to repeal Standard 206, which had already been suspended since February 2025.

item.title: Treasury hails success of anti-fraud process that flagged and prevented $99 million in payments to dead people
item.description: The Treasury Department screened 885 million federal payments against death records, stopping $99 million from being sent to deceased recipients.
item.content:encoded:
The Treasury Department announced Tuesday morning that it has successfully implemented new safeguards to prevent government payments to dead people in an effort to reduce waste and fraud.

The verification system includes screening 885 million federal payments against expanded death records, effectively auditing payments totaling nearly $2.7 trillion. It has already flagged and stopped payments totaling roughly $99 million that had been marked to be sent to dead people.

"Treasury has delivered on a key promise of President Trump’s mandate to stop improper payments and fraud before money leaves the Treasury, and strengthen the integrity of the federal payment system," Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said in a statement. "Together with Vice President Vance’s Task Force to Eliminate Fraud, this new safeguard addresses a longstanding vulnerability and helps ensure every dollar the federal government spends reaches its intended recipient. Treasury will continue efforts to modernize the federal payment system, strengthen safeguards against fraud and improper payments, and protect taxpayer dollars."

ELON MUSK SAYS MILLIONS IN SOCIAL SECURITY DATABASE ARE BETWEEN AGES OF 100 AND 159

The move tallies a victory for President Donald Trump's March 2025 Executive Order 14249, "Protecting America's Bank Account Against Fraud, Waste, and Abuse."

More than 4,900 payments linked to deceased payees were returned to the federal agencies that initiated them for further review.

The effort relies in part on Treasury’s expanded access to the Social Security Administration’s Full Death Master File, which contains death information used to identify potentially ineligible recipients before payments are issued.

Congress initially gave Treasury temporary access to the database under a three-year pilot program authorized by the Consolidated Appropriations Act of 2021. Treasury projected the program would produce about $330 million in net benefits from 2024 through 2026 by reducing improper payments.

TRUMP ELECTION INTEGRITY PUSH EXPOSES MASSIVE AMOUNT OF DEAD PEOPLE ON NORTH CAROLINA VOTER ROLLS

Access was made permanent in February 2026, when Congress passed and Trump signed the Ending Improper Payments to Deceased People Act, introduced by Sen. John Kennedy, R-La.

Treasury said it will continue expanding the verification system across the federal government as part of a broader effort to prevent questionable payments before taxpayer money leaves federal accounts.

Elon Musk, when he started the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) in the first month of the second Trump administration, famously called out the more than 20 million listed on Social Security with ages 100 and higher, including more than 3.9 million in the 130-139 age range, more than 3.5 million in the 140-149 range and more than 1.3 million in the 150-159 range.

"According to the Social Security database, these are the numbers of people in each age bucket with the death field set to FALSE! Maybe Twilight is real and there are a lot of vampires collecting Social Security," Musk joked on X in February 2025.

The Vance-led effort to root out government waste, fraud and abuse will have more developments to report Tuesday as Health and Human Services (HHS) Secretary Robert F. Kennedy, Jr. and Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) Administrator Dr. Mehmet Oz will join leaders from the White House Anti-Fraud Task Force and the HHS Office of Inspector General (OIG) for an 11 a.m. news conference on new federal actions to protect taxpayer dollars.

item.title: Shania Twain says she's finally 'getting it right' in her 60s after starting life ‘on the tough side’
item.description: Shania Twain opened up about how childhood poverty, stage fright and a Lyme disease battle shaped her journey to finding peace in her 60s.
item.content:encoded:
Shania Twain is looking back on her long journey from an impoverished childhood in rural Canada to international superstardom and finally coming to terms with her past in her 60s.

During a recent interview with "CBS Mornings," the 60-year-old singer opened up about her difficult upbringing and how she has gained a new outlook later in life.

"I started life out a little bit on the tough side," Twain said. "In my 60s now, I feel like I've really gotten it, or I'm just now getting all of that right."

When asked by CBS correspondent Seth Doane about why her 60s had given her that perspective, Twain had a simple answer.

SHANIA TWAIN'S LIQUID DIET HELPS HER AVOID A ‘NIGHTMARE’ CONCERT MISHAP

"You know what it is? It's called time," she said. "I'd love to have been able to get it right in my 50s, but I just – that wasn't enough time. I needed to be in my 60s to start getting it right, you know?"

Born Eilleen Regina Edwards in Windsor, Ontario, Twain was raised in the small town of Timmins by her mother, Sharon, and adoptive father Jerry Twain after her biological parents divorced when she was 2 years old.

TAYLOR HICKS CREDITS FAITH AND BLUE-COLLAR BIRMINGHAM ROOTS FOR KEEPING HIM GROUNDED AFTER 'AMERICAN IDOL' WIN

Twain's family struggled financially, and the singer has spoken candidly about growing up in poverty and witnessing domestic abuse at home.

While speaking with "CBS Mornings," Twain recalled that she was forced to grow up quickly, taking on burdens that far exceeded those of a typical child.

"When I was younger, I was so over-responsible for the household," she remembered. "My mother was depressed a lot of the time. She spent a lot of her days in bed. She lived off coffee and cigarettes that I would bring her in bed just to see if she was still alive."

VANILLA ICE SAYS HE NEARLY JOINED THE ‘27 CLUB’ BEFORE QUITTING DRUGS FOR GOOD

"There was a lot of violence in the house," Twain added. "And we didn't eat well a lot of the times. We weren't clothed properly a lot of the time."

Long before becoming one of country music's biggest stars, Twain performed in bars as a child to help support her family, including her mother, stepfather and four siblings.

The singer shared that her early performing years led her to develop stage fright, which would plague her for most of her career regardless of her audience size.

"The amount of people is not necessarily what affected me over the years with stage fright," Twain said. "It was starting so young in bars that were, you know, I was 8 years old, and the bars were smoky, and everyone was intoxicated. I don't know what I'm actually singing about, because I'm singing about divorce, and I'm eight."

Twain explained that she did not realize how deeply those experiences had shaped her until much later in life.

"This was just an uncomfortable place to be as a child, and I carried that with me," she said. "And only in my 50s, I finally just said, 'Whoa. I've got my voice. I have an audience. I have everything to just celebrate.' And the fear just dropped away."

Twain's challenges did not end with her difficult childhood. In 1987, when she was 22, her mother and stepfather were killed in a car accident. While both Twain and her older sister Jill were already adults, their three younger siblings, including sister Carrie-Ann and brothers Mark and Darry, were underage and needed a legal guardian.

At the time, Twain was trying to establish herself as a singer-songwriter after moving to Toronto to pursue music. However, Twain put her career on hold and moved back to Ontario to care for her younger siblings.

During a 2023 appearance on "The Drew Barrymore Show," Twain recalled the difficulties that she faced after becoming her siblings' primary caregiver.

GOLDIE HAWN THINKS KIDS LACK 'GRIT' FROM TOO MUCH SCREEN TIME, NOT ENOUGH REAL-WORLD PROBLEM SOLVING

"That was a different type of parenting because now there was nobody to blame because now they’re gone," she said. "All of a sudden, I’m responsible for making decisions about my parents’ mortgage. I didn’t know they had a mortgage, I didn’t know what it was, I didn’t know anything about how they ran their business or debts or insurance. I had to learn overnight. My parents died — three days later I’m in lawyer’s offices and insurance company offices."

Twain told "CBS Mornings" that with the benefit of time, she is now able to look back on those experiences with gratitude instead of focusing solely on the hardship.

"When I reflect back on those times, I'm reflecting on them from where I am now, which is a very good place," Twain said. "Like, I made it out of there. It's like, 'Whoa, I'm just celebrating all of that.' And what makes me feel so accomplished, is staying connected to where I came from. It's the contrast that makes it feel so good."

After her siblings were older and living independently, Twain finally turned her attention back to her own dreams. In 1991, she moved to Nashville and signed a recording deal with the legendary country music label Mercury Nashville.

Twain's breakthrough came in 1995 with the release of her Grammy-winning sophomore album, "The Woman in Me." Two years later, she skyrocketed to global fame with "Come On Over," the best-selling album by a female artist of all time.

JANE SEYMOUR, 75, SHARES HOW SHE APPROACHES AGING WITH VITALITY: 'BEST I CAN BE'

Over the course of her career, Twain has sold more than 100 million records worldwide, won five Grammy Awards and cemented her status as a country music icon.

However, Twain faced new obstacles even after reaching superstardom, including divorce and a battle with Lyme disease.

In 2003, she was diagnosed with Lyme disease, which affected her vocal cords and threatened her singing career. The ordeal contributed to a 15-year gap between studio albums before she returned with 2017's "Now," which debuted at No. 1 on the Billboard 200 chart.

In 2018, Twain underwent open-throat surgery and returned to touring. During her appearance on "CBS Mornings," she reflected on regaining her voice after her health struggles.

"Once I made a sound, it was just there," Twain recalled. "I feel lucky. I don't want to make it sound easy! It wasn't easy, it was a very long road, but I just feel very grateful and very lucky that I have it back."

"I have more, like, of a fry to my voice, you know, a little gravel," she continued. "I don't mind that. I've embraced it. It's now part of my sound."

Twain explained that she has adapted to the changes in her voice since resuming her career.

"I've taken a different approach to singing my older songs," she said. "In fact, I've found new placements in my voice that I enjoy, you know, that I didn't have before."

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR THE ENTERTAINMENT NEWSLETTER

"I love to experiment. I think it's the same with any art form," Twain added. "You have to experiment a little bit to get just the right – whatever's satisfying you the most at that time, you know?"

Twain recently fulfilled a longtime dream when she surprised fans with a performance at Toronto's legendary Horseshoe Tavern.

After taking the stage on Friday night, she told the crowd, "I grew up in Northern Ontario and the dream in my little bar bands at the time, all through my youth and my teens, even in my 20s, was to play the Horseshoe Tavern."

"But we weren’t recognized … we just weren’t established enough, so we never made the hire here," she continued. "So this is my first time in the Horseshoe Tavern."

LIKE WHAT YOU'RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE ENTERTAINMENT NEWS

"I guess I’ve finally made it after all these years," Twain added as the crowd cheered. "It was really a big deal to be able to play the Horseshoe Tavern. We all talked about that up North, ‘Oh, maybe someday we can play the Horseshoe Tavern.’ And now I’m playing here. I feel like it’s a very full-circle moment,"

"My home province, with all you beautiful people, in this historic bar. This is so historical. So it’s a privilege to be here, finally."

While speaking with "CBS Mornings," Twain explained how viewing her past struggles through a new lens had inspired her new album "Little Miss Twain."

"'Little Miss Twain' is me, now, looking back at my child's self and the journey that child went through, from starting out in those bars at 8 years old, right up to when I got my recording contract in Nashville," she said.

Though she acknowledged that it took decades to feel like she had"gotten it right," Twain said she is content with the timing.

"That's fine," she said. "I plan to live to be 200 anyway!"

item.title: Startling mystery revealed in centuries-old royal tombs at powerful queen's monastery
item.description: Several royal tombs at Pedralbes Monastery contained the wrong people, with 25 remains found in eight tombs and DNA testing planned through 2027.
item.content:encoded:
When researchers in Barcelona recently opened centuries-old royal tombs, they uncovered a startling mystery: Several contained the wrong people.

The exhumations took place at the Royal Monastery of Santa Maria de Pedralbes in Barcelona, Spain, a nearly 700-year-old Gothic monastery founded by Queen Elisenda of Montcada.

The wife of King James II of Aragon, Elisenda, founded the monastery in 1326 — and to mark its 700th anniversary, researchers wrapped up a multidisciplinary investigation into its medieval tombs.

HIDDEN UNDERGROUND CHAMBER REVEALED BENEATH HOME OF LEGENDARY ROMAN EMPEROR

Local officials have now shared the first results of a three-year excavation and research project, which produced several unexpected discoveries.

They found that many of the remains did not belong to the people assigned to the tombs.

"One of the project's most significant findings is that the opening of several tombs showed that the identities historically assigned to them do not necessarily correspond with the remains found inside," the release noted, which was translated from Catalan.

"In the tomb of Artau de Foces, researchers found no male individual. Instead, they discovered the remains of five people: two adult women and three children."

The sheer number of skeletons found was surprising, said Enric Puga, communications officer for the Royal Monastery of Santa Maria de Pedralbes.

1,740-YEAR-OLD ROMAN COIN BEARING EMPEROR'S NAME FOUND AS ENGLAND RAIL DIG REVEALS CLUES

Puga told Fox News Digital that researchers were not expecting to find the remains of 25 people in eight tombs — and were even more surprised to find over 200 archaeological samples, including funerary offerings and preserved plants.

"The openings have shown re-openings and subsequent alterations, which means that we do not always find the person identified with the tomb," he said.

"This is the case of the tomb of Francesca Saportella, second abbess of the monastery, where at least nine individuals of late chronologies have been found, some with edge weapon wounds, as well as a female trunk mummified with fetal remains," added Puga.

"The abbess is not there, in any of the identified remains."

MYSTERY DEEPENS AFTER 2,700-YEAR-OLD BRONZE SWORD EMERGES STANDING UPRIGHT

He said it was "reassuring" to find that the wooden box preserving the queen's tomb dated to the medieval period and remained in excellent condition.

Researchers found that Queen Elisenda was buried in simple, modest clothing, despite being a queen — and that her remains showed health conditions commonly associated with old age.

"Thanks to archaeobotanical analysis, we have been able to identify several different funerary rites, with plants associated with body preparation, such as rosemary, but also floral offerings with a symbolic component," he added.

TEST YOURSELF WITH OUR LATEST LIFESTYLE QUIZ

"The subsequent reopening of some tombs gives us a lot of information about the funerary culture. ... Inside some tombs, some wrapped in bundles, we have found many objects from the daily life of the period."

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR OUR LIFESTYLE NEWSLETTER

Additional DNA testing is planned through 2027.

So far, researchers have recovered about 6% of Queen Elisenda's genome, allowing them to make preliminary estimates about some of her pigmentation traits, according to the project.

Puga added that all the research was done with the approval of the monastery's community of nuns.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE LIFESTYLE NEWS

"However, [Queen Elisenda's] final will [shall] always be honored, which stipulates that all remains studied must be returned to the monastery to be interred in the same location where they were originally buried," said Puga.

Overall, Puga said the results have provided "significant insights regarding the 14th century."

"Once all the samples have been analyzed, this information on the burials of a female power center will enable an understanding of medieval Catalan society," he said.
